baseline_kernel:
// G=64 groups, C/G=4 channels per group
// 64 separate tiny convolutions
analysis: ShuffleNet uses high group count for efficiency
Each group is tiny: 4 input → 4 output channels
Channel shuffle between layers restores mixing
schedule_reasoning: Smaller 64×64 tile for tiny per-group problem
Alignment=4 matches channels per group
Batch all 64 groups into single kernel
Grid includes group dimension: (tiles, tiles, N×G)
concept: Grouped conv G=64 - ShuffleNet efficient groups

baseline_kernel:
// G=2: original AlexNet split across 2 GPUs
// Each group: C/2=256 → K/2=512
analysis: AlexNet historical: split model across 2 GTX 580s
Each GPU processed half the channels
Now legacy but still used in some architectures
schedule_reasoning: Large tile - each group is substantial (256→512)
Only 2 groups - low group overhead
Channels per group (256) >> tensor core minimum
Grid: (tiles_h, tiles_w, N×2)

baseline_kernel:
// Depthwise: each channel has own 3×3 filter
// K dimension = 9, way below tensor core threshold
analysis: MobileNet depthwise: each of 32 channels filtered independently
Implicit GEMM K = R×S = 9 << tensor core minimum 16
Direct convolution more efficient for tiny K
schedule_reasoning: Direct convolution: explicit 3×3 loop
3×3=9 filter elements fit in registers
Each thread processes multiple spatial positions
No tensor cores - compute bound anyway
concept: Depthwise MobileNet - direct conv for C=32

baseline_kernel:
// 5×5 depthwise: K = 25, still below threshold
// But more filter elements than 3×3
analysis: 5×5 filter: 25 taps per channel
Slightly better arithmetic intensity than 3×3
Still not enough for tensor cores (need 32+)
schedule_reasoning: 5×5=25 filter elements in shared memory
Larger tile since 512 channels
Shared memory filter: less register pressure
Still direct algorithm - tensor cores not viable

baseline_kernel:
// First layer: C=3 (RGB)
// Standard iterator loads 32 bytes, uses only 3×2=6 bytes
analysis: RGB input: only 3 channels
Standard 32-byte load uses 6 bytes (2B per f16)
kFewChannels reorganizes to pack spatial positions
schedule_reasoning: kFewChannels packs multiple H,W positions per load
Instead of loading C=3 padded to 16, loads 10+ pixels × 3 channels
Alignment=2 matches actual data (no padding)
Critical for ResNet first conv performance
concept: Few channels C=3 - RGB ResNet stem

baseline_kernel:
// C=4: RGBA image input
// int8 MMA needs K=32, but C×R×S = 4×9 = 36
analysis: RGBA input: 4 channels (RGB + alpha)
int8 implicit GEMM K = C × R × S = 36
Just above int8 tensor core K=32 threshold
schedule_reasoning: kFewChannels still useful - 4 channels not efficiently packed otherwise
int8 16×8×32 MMA
K=64 tile > single filter window (36)
Batches partial filter windows for efficiency
concept: Few channels C=4 int8 - RGBA quantized inference

baseline_kernel:
// C=32 known at compile time
// Can fully unroll channel loop
analysis: C=32: exactly matches tensor core K-dimension
kFixedChannels hardcodes C at compile time
Compiler can fully unroll and optimize
schedule_reasoning: kFixedChannels with C=32 (compile-time constant)
Loop fully unrolled: no bounds checks
Register allocation optimal: exactly 32 channels fit
Used when channel count is architectural constant

baseline_kernel:
// C=64: common ResNet intermediate
// Fixed channels enables deeper optimization
analysis: C=64: very common dimension (64, 128, 256, 512)
kFixedChannels specializes for C=64
2× K-tiles per filter window: 64×9=576 → 576/32=18 tiles
schedule_reasoning: C=64 hardcoded: full loop unrolling possible
4 stages to pipeline multiple K-tiles per window
Common pattern: generate separate kernels for C=64,128,256,512
Faster than single generic kernel
concept: Fixed channels C=64 - ResNet intermediate layer

baseline_kernel:
// Maxwell/Pascal: no tensor cores
// Pure SIMT: each thread computes scalar FMAs
analysis: SM50 (Maxwell) has no tensor cores
SIMT GEMM: each thread owns output elements
Shared memory for A,B tile reuse
schedule_reasoning: 128×128 output tile per threadblock
K=8 tile depth: 128×8 + 8×128 = 2KB A,B tiles
256 threads: each computes 64 output elements
SIMT baseline for architectures < SM70

baseline_kernel:
// N=1, huge H×W: single image, many spatial positions
// Offset table scales with H×W
analysis: Optimized iterator precomputes offset table: O(H×W) entries
For 1024×1024 image: 1M offsets × 4B = 4MB
Analytic iterator: compute offsets on-the-fly, no table
schedule_reasoning: kAnalytic computes offsets dynamically
No O(H×W) precomputed table
Trade compute (offset calculation) for memory savings
Better for single large images, worse for batched small

baseline_kernel:
// f64 SYMM: C = A*B where A is symmetric
// Need full f64 precision for HPC
analysis: f64 symmetric multiply for: finite elements, physics
A stored as lower triangle: A[i,j] = A[j,i]
DMMA tensor cores for f64 acceleration
schedule_reasoning: 64×64 tile for f64 register pressure
DMMA 8×8×4 f64 tensor cores
Left side: C = A × B
Lower fill: A's lower triangle stored
concept: SYMM f64 - double precision for FEM/physics
